exemplar.title:
A Replication of Stroop (1935):
A Study on Interference Theory Regarding Colours and Serial Verbal Reactions

A Replication of Stroop (1935):
A Study on Interference Theory Regarding Colours and Serial Verbal Reactions

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, focused aim with well-explained relevance to the target population
Comprehensive description of interference theory and explicit linkage to the experiment
Fully operationalised directional hypothesis with precise IV and DV measurements
weaknesses: Null hypothesis wording is overly verbose and could be more concise
Relevance discussion would benefit from additional empirical citations (e.g., on media multitasking in teens)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three introduction strands clearly meet the 5â6 descriptor with well-developed explanations and only minor areas for refinement. A top-band mark is justified, but slight weaknesses in null-hypothesis phrasing and broader citation support warrant a score of 5 rather than 6.

strengths: Repeated-measures design is not only described but well explained in terms of controlling variability and facilitating causal inference
Volunteer sampling rationale (speed, convenience, biases) is clearly articulated
Controlled variables and material choices (quiet room, PowerPoint presentation, word list) are justified for replicability
weaknesses: Discussion omits potential practice effects and how to manage them
Generalizability considerations (e.g., sample demographics) could be deeper
Procedural detail could specify ambient noise levels for fuller replicability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All exploration strands align with the 3â4 band, showing thorough explanation rather than mere description. Minor gaps in addressing practice effects and demographic impact prevent a perfect score, yielding a score of 4.

strengths: Appropriate selection and accurate execution of descriptive and inferential statistics (related-samples t-test)
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with clear labels and units
Interpretation explicitly links statistical findings to hypothesis and interference theory
weaknesses: Graph lacks error bars to represent variability
Standard deviation rationale is cut off, leaving the choice only partially explained
Ordering of inferential and descriptive statistics in the write-up is premature
One-tailed test justification needs explicit rationale
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis, graphing, and interpretation all sit in the top band but multiple execution and reporting weaknesses suggest a slight underperformance against the 5â6 descriptor. A score of 4 reflects strong foundation with room for improvement.

strengths: Findings are discussed in depth with reference to interference theory and selective attention
Strengths and limitations are relevant, explained, and tied to validity/reliability
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and well justified
weaknesses: Justification for reversing word order is truncated
Evaluation would benefit from effect-size discussion (e.g., Cohenâs d)
Real-world implications (e.g., classroom practice) are not fully integrated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All evaluation strands meet the 5â6 descriptor through thorough discussion, rationale, and linkage. However, truncated text and missing depth in implication and effect-size evaluation warrant a slight reduction, resulting in a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A clear bar graph with properly labelled axes, units and values directly compares conditions and addresses the hypothesis. Presentation is accurate and free of flaws, fulfilling the 5-6 descriptor.

The student explicitly states the statistical outcome, links it to rejection of H0 and ties it back to colour-interference theory, demonstrating full interpretation with regard to both data and hypothesis â meeting top-band criteria.

Both IV (list congruency) and DV (reaction time in seconds for 30 words) are clearly embedded in the directional hypothesis, including how each is measured, so the variables are fully operationalised â meeting the 5-6 standard.
Volunteer sampling is defined and its speed, convenience and likely biases are discussed, showing an explanatory rationale for participant choice; therefore it belongs in the 3-4 band.

Interference theory, proactive inhibition and the Stroop effect are accurately described and the link between automatic colour processing and the present experiment is explicitly spelled out. The explanation is coherent and error-free, satisfying the top-band requirement.

Results are not only described but discussed in the context of interference theory, selective attention and automaticity, comparing replication with Stroopâs original work. This constitutes a genuine discussion rather than mere description, fitting 5-6.

The student states a clear aim â to test whether incongruent colour-word pairs slow reading time â and explicitly explains its educational relevance for 16-18-year-olds and study skills. This moves past mere identification into a reasoned explanation, fully matching the 5-6 descriptor.

Multiple relevant strengths (counterbalancing, standardisation) and limitations (self-selection bias, expectancy effects) are explained in relation to validity and reliability, satisfying the âexplained and relevantâ requirement of the top band.

Key controls (quiet room, non-psychology students) and material changes (PowerPoint, 30 words) are mentioned with some justification, though depth is slightly thinner than for design; still, the student explains choices, warranting low end of 3-4.

Each suggested modification (reversing word order, random sampling, printed sheets) is explicitly tied to a named limitation and justified in terms of validity or reliability, fully matching the highest descriptor for this strand.

The student not only describes the repeated-measures design but explains why it controls participant variability and facilitates causal inference, thus exceeding description and fitting the âexplainedâ level (3-4).

Interval data, a related-samples t-test and descriptive measures are correctly selected and executed; p-value, t and df are appropriate. No calculation errors are evident, so the statistical work is both appropriate and accurate â top band.

annotations.comment: The student provides thorough historical context by integrating primary sources from Stroop (1935) and earlier theorists, demonstrating a strong foundation in interference theory description.

The linkage between Stroopâs original findings and the proactive interference framework is clearly articulated, reinforcing the theoretical justification for the incongruent vs. congruent conditions.
Relevance discussion links colour interference to study skills, but could be strengthened by citing empirical studies on media multitasking effects in teens for additional support.
The directional hypothesis operationalises the IV and DV with precise measurement details (reaction time in seconds for 30 words), ensuring a testable prediction.
The repeated measures design is well explained for controlling participant variability; adding a note on managing potential practice effects would further strengthen the design rationale.
Volunteer sampling is described clearly; the explanation would benefit from addressing how sample demographics (e.g., gender balance) might influence generalisability.
Controls (quiet room, non-psychology students) are justified; consider specifying ambient noise levels or decibel range to enhance procedural replicability.
The ethical procedure is robust, with informed consent and debrief steps clearly outlined, demonstrating adherence to IB guidelines.
Fig. 1 is well presented with correctly labelled axes; adding error bars would visually represent variability and improve interpretation.
The explanation of interval data and absence of outliers is clear, demonstrating correct selection of descriptive measures for reaction time data.
The title clearly conveys the focus on Stroop replication and interference theory, enhancing reader orientation and immediate comprehension of the studyâs scope.
The null hypothesis is stated, but its wording is verbose. Consider condensing to improve clarity and focus on the comparison between means.
The interpretation of mean differences and immediate rejection of Hâ based on means alone is premature; inferential statistics should be presented first.
The description of standard deviation is cut off midâsentence; ensure full explanation of why SD was chosen over other dispersion measures.
The rationale for the related-samples t-test is well articulated, showing correct alignment between design, data level, and statistical choice.
The one-tailed p-value is reported correctly; however, justify the use of a directional test to strengthen inferential validity.
Discussion of findings links results to interference theory and Stroopâs originals, demonstrating highâband evaluation of theory integration.
The student references selective attention and practice effects well, deepening the theoretical discussion beyond Stroopâs original conclusions.
The modification suggestion on reversing list order is truncated; ensure complete justification linking this change to the specific limitation.
The strength of counterbalancing is well noted, but including effect sizes (e.g., Cohenâs d) would deepen evaluation of design effectiveness.
The material modification rationale is clear; consider critically discussing whether digital presentation versus paper affects cognitive load and ecological validity.
The conclusion restates results but could integrate implications for classroom practice or study strategies to enhance real-world application.
The table of contents is comprehensive, but page numbers for the 'Sample' and 'Materials' entries do not match actual pagination; verify accuracy.
Procedure steps are clearly listed and counterbalancing instructions are detailed, supporting replication potential and procedural transparency.
The axis caption correctly identifies the variable and units; including the t-test result (t, df, p) in the figure caption would improve standalone interpretability.
